title: Making AI Work for Cyber Defenders: A Strategy for Strengthening U.S. Cybersecurity
gist: CSIS argues frontier AI models like Anthropic's Mythos and OpenAI's GPT-5.5-Cyber have already demonstrated hours-scale breaches of classified US systems, and lays out a two-part strategy — AI-enabled 'monitor and swiftly repair' cyber defense plus mandatory 30-day pre-release and broader post-release frontier-model reviews — to keep pace with a China that assessments suggest is under a year behind.

themes.theme: Frontier AI has changed the offense-defense balance

insights.claim: With sufficient coordination, AI can transform US cyber defense by letting defenders detect, evaluate, remediate and harden systems at machine speed, but this will not happen automatically — it requires deliberate, rapid action by government, the private sector and nonprofits to enable safe, widespread deployment of these tools.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The AI-cyber-defense upside is conditional, not inevitable — investors/policymakers should track actual deployment and coordination milestones, not just model capability announcements.
insights.quote: Like other technological advances with the potential to strengthen cybersecurity, AI will not automatically bolster defenders.
insights.locator: Executive Summary

insights.claim: Most assessments suggest China either already has frontier AI models with remarkable cyber capabilities comparable to the newest US models, or will develop them in slightly less than a year.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Sets a roughly one-year window for the US to harden defenses before Chinese state and criminal actors gain equivalent offensive AI cyber capability.
insights.locator: Executive Summary

insights.claim: In spring 2026, Anthropic's Mythos demonstrated the ability to identify and exploit previously unseen flaws in long-standing essential software, and reportedly proved capable of breaching the US government's most sensitive classified systems within hours; OpenAI's GPT-5.5-Cyber separately uncovered and used similar vulnerabilities.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A concrete, named benchmark of how fast frontier-model cyber capability has advanced — classified-system compromise in hours, not weeks.
insights.quote: The model later reportedly proved capable of breaching the U.S. government's most sensitive classified systems in a matter of “hours.”
insights.locator: Introduction

insights.claim: Sophisticated threat actors are increasing autonomous and semiautonomous attacks by coordinating swarms of AI agents through advanced LLMs, as Anthropic documented in a November 2025 report, allowing simultaneous attacks against targets worldwide.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: AI-agent-swarm attacks are already documented in the field, not hypothetical — defenders should assume simultaneous, multi-target campaigns rather than isolated intrusions.
insights.locator: The AI-Enabled Cyber Era: Changes in Degree and Kind of Cyberattacks

insights.claim: Cyber threat actors can boost attack effectiveness either by jailbreaking closed proprietary models (whose safety constraints are fallible) or by using open-weight models, which are widely available and either lack such constraints or have constraints that are easily removed.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Open-weight model proliferation is itself a cybersecurity policy variable, not just an AI-safety one — argues for scrutiny of open-weight release practices.
insights.locator: Cyber threat actors could use LLMs to improve attack effectiveness

insights.claim: AI-generated code introduces new vulnerabilities in over 10 percent of its output, according to some experts, a risk set to grow as AI is used increasingly to draft and implement production code.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Quantifies a direct, measurable tech-debt/security risk from AI-assisted coding adoption that code-review and monitoring tooling must specifically target.
insights.locator: Monitoring strategies usually include AI agents that scan newly written code

themes.theme: AI-enabled defense: monitor and swiftly repair

insights.claim: The central imperative for defenders is ensuring they can seamlessly and fully leverage AI-enabled capabilities to uncover, prioritize and mitigate vulnerabilities — if defenders instead rely on traditional human-speed workflows, malicious actors will find and exploit vulnerabilities faster than legitimate users can patch them.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Frames patch-cycle speed as the decisive variable in the AI cyber arms race, not model capability alone.
insights.quote: if defenders continue to primarily rely on traditional workflows based on human speed, or insufficiently incorporate AI tools, then malicious actors will be able to find and exploit vulnerabilities faster than legitimate users can develop and deploy patches
insights.locator: Executive Summary

insights.claim: The US should shift its cybersecurity posture, building on the NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0's six core functions (govern, identify, protect, detect, respond, recover), toward AI-augmented vigilant monitoring for attacks/vulnerabilities and swift repair, with general-purpose patches released open source and bespoke patches developed for specific vulnerabilities.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete organizing framework (NIST CSF 2.0) that vendors and agencies can map AI-defense products/services against.
insights.locator: Persistently Monitoring and Swiftly Repairing Infrastructure

insights.claim: Credible AI-enabled cyber-defense prototypes and early operational deployments show AI can speed monitoring and some repairs, but the strongest results remain in controlled pilot settings rather than broad infrastructure rollouts, meaning there is a severe vulnerability window before AI defenses are fully and consistently implemented.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Tempers hype around AI cyber-defense readiness — current capability is pilot-stage, so near-term risk of attackers outpacing legacy defensive processes is real and should be priced in.
insights.locator: As described, there is promising research and prototyping regarding AI-enabled cyber defense

insights.claim: Free and open-source software maintainers, small and medium-sized businesses, and legacy/industrial control systems (municipal water systems, electric utilities) will lag in adopting AI-enabled defenses without dedicated funding and incentives, because these less-resourced sectors lack money, urgency and technical expertise — a persistent structural weak point in national cyber resilience.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Identifies critical infrastructure and open-source software as the systemic tail risk in an AI-accelerated threat environment — a case for targeted public/private subsidy rather than market-driven adoption alone.
insights.locator: Without incentives from the executive branch and Congress, improved cybersecurity practices will spread slowly

themes.theme: Governing frontier models: pre- and post-release review

insights.claim: The report recommends a mandatory 30-day pre-release government review of new frontier models trained above a specified compute threshold, focused on roughly 10-15 dangerous substantive capability areas (e.g., end-to-end cyberattacks, catastrophic biological weapons, a 10-kiloton nuclear weapon) and roughly 10-15 misaligned-behavior categories (deceptive alignment, oversight evasion, sabotage, self-preservation/resistance to correction).
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Gives investors/operators a concrete regulatory-timeline expectation (30 days) for model releases, plus the specific capability categories likely to trigger delay or restriction.
insights.locator: Pre-Release Review

insights.claim: Compute is used as the practical trigger for mandatory review because it is a good proxy for a balanced mix of computing power, data and parameters; overall model capability grows smoothly and predictably with these inputs, but the distribution of that growth across specific skills is "jagged" and cannot be reliably predicted in advance — which the report suggests explains why Mythos's cyberattack capability jumped so much from its predecessor.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Explains why capability audits, not just aggregate benchmark scores, are necessary — a single dangerous skill can spike even when overall model improvement looks incremental.
insights.locator: This strange characteristic of frontier models may explain why the cyberattack capabilities of Mythos increased so much

insights.claim: The report argues that zero-day vulnerabilities discovered during frontier-model pre-release review should not be preserved by the national security or intelligence community for their own offensive use, breaking from historical practice, because the pace of Chinese frontier-model development means any vulnerability a US review discovers is likely to be found by a strategic competitor within a few months.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A direct policy break from past NSA/intelligence zero-day-hoarding practice — argues for immediate patch-and-disclose given the compressed competitive timeline with China.
insights.locator: this report recommends that no cyber vulnerabilities discovered as part of this review process be preserved for that purpose

insights.claim: Post-release review should be broader and less formal than pre-release review, drawing on three funding pathways: additional government funding for academic researchers, government or philanthropic funding for NGO-based technical analysis, and a new government-funded analytical organization (potentially an FFRDC) with safe-harbor access to proprietary training databases, algorithms and model weights.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Signals coming demand for independent AI-safety analytical capacity outside the frontier labs themselves — relevant to academic/NGO funding flows and a prospective new FFRDC contract.
insights.locator: Post-Release Review

themes.theme: Building the institutions: Treasury, CISA, CAISI and an FFRDC

insights.claim: As of the June 2026 White House executive order, the Department of the Treasury is designated as the lead for a voluntary US government AI-cybersecurity clearinghouse coordinating detection, verification and remediation of software vulnerabilities, with the Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) within NIST and CISA serving as coordinating centers of governmental AI expertise.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific agencies (Treasury, CAISI, CISA) currently holding AI-cybersecurity coordination authority — the relevant counterparties for industry engagement today.
insights.locator: Today, the Department of the Treasury is the designated lead

insights.claim: The report recommends CISA lead design of a new cyber-breach tracking system aligned with CIRCIA reporting requirements, with routine data collection and consolidation shifted to a new federally funded research and development center (FFRDC) for continuity and technical expertise, working with NIST to align with international incident-reporting standards.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Points to a likely new FFRDC contracting opportunity/vehicle in breach-data management — relevant for national labs and contractors positioning for that role.
insights.locator: Breach Notification

insights.claim: The Office of Management and Budget should mandate a baseline enterprise software security requirement, developed in consultation with companies participating in Project Glasswing and other industry groups, for all software purchased by the federal government and its contractors starting six months after the criteria are established.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A concrete, dated federal procurement mandate that would force software vendors and contractors to meet a new AI-era security baseline within a six-month compliance window.
insights.locator: Federal Software Procurement

insights.claim: NIST is already expanding its work on tailored AI system security control overlays and engaging companies with the most advanced frontier-model capabilities — currently named as Mythos and GPT-5.5 — around setting a baseline for federal government software purchases.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Confirms direct government engagement is already underway with the two named leading labs on security baselines, ahead of any formal legislative mandate.
insights.locator: NIST is currently expanding its work developing tailored control overlays

insights.claim: In the long term, an effective US strategy must pair Treasury's near-term clearinghouse work with a law codifying executive branch roles and sustained investment building institutional expertise across all federal agencies responsible for each of the 16 designated critical infrastructure sectors.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Signals that current ad hoc coordination (Treasury-led clearinghouse) is a bridge, not an end-state — expect future legislative action to formalize agency roles.
insights.locator: Long-Term Role for the Executive Branch

themes.theme: Public legitimacy and civil society's role

insights.claim: Public opposition to the construction of data centers needed for US compute capacity shows that the AI-benefits-and-harms dialogue must occur in the public domain, not solely between technology companies and the executive branch.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Frames data-center siting/public opposition as a live constraint on the compute buildout that underpins both frontier-model development and AI-cyber-defense capacity.
insights.locator: As demonstrated by public opposition to the construction of data centers

insights.claim: Large technology companies have significant self-interest and resources to upgrade their own cyber defenses and those of their users, and a private coalition ideally catalyzed by the executive branch should help secure the more fragile parts of the ecosystem — open-source software, SMBs, and legacy industrial control systems.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Argues the executive branch's highest-leverage near-term move is convening/catalyzing a private-sector coalition rather than funding everything directly.
insights.locator: Large technology companies, in any case, would have significant self-interest
